04 State Management — Context, Zustand, Jotai, and the Signals Debate

← Back to lessons

Most apps over-engineer state. You don't need Redux for a todo app. You probably don't need Zustand either. This lesson maps the decision tree: what kind of state is it, how many components need it, and what's the simplest tool that works.

The State Taxonomy: Not All State Is Created Equal

Before choosing a tool, categorize what you're storing. Different categories have different solutions:

CategoryExamplesBest Tool
Local UI stateModal open/closed, form input, accordion expandeduseState
Computed/derivedFiltered list, total price, formatted dateJust compute it (useMemo if expensive)
Server stateUser profile, articles, API responsesReact Query / SWR / RSC
URL stateSearch params, pagination, filtersuseSearchParams / URL
Shared client stateTheme, auth, shopping cart, notificationsContext, Zustand, or Jotai
Form stateField values, validation errors, dirty trackingReact Hook Form (lesson 09)

Most "state management" problems are actually server state problems. If you're putting API responses in Redux or Zustand, you're reinventing what React Query does automatically: caching, invalidation, background refetching, loading/error states. Move server data to React Query and you'll find 60-80% of your "global state" disappears.

Context API: When and How

Context solves one specific problem: passing data through the component tree without prop drilling. That's it. It's not a state manager — it's a dependency injection mechanism.

// Creating and providing context
type Theme = 'light' | 'dark';

const ThemeContext = createContext<{
  theme: Theme;
  toggle: () => void;
} | null>(null);

function ThemeProvider({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) {
  const [theme, setTheme] = useState<Theme>('dark');
  const toggle = useCallback(
    () => setTheme(t => t === 'dark' ? 'light' : 'dark'),
    []
  );

  // useMemo prevents all consumers re-rendering when provider re-renders
  const value = useMemo(() => ({ theme, toggle }), [theme, toggle]);

  return (
    <ThemeContext.Provider value={value}>
      {children}
    </ThemeContext.Provider>
  );
}

// Custom hook for consuming (type-safe)
function useTheme() {
  const ctx = useContext(ThemeContext);
  if (!ctx) throw new Error('useTheme must be used within ThemeProvider');
  return ctx;
}

Context's re-render problem

Here's the gotcha: when a context value changes, every component that calls useContext re-renders. There's no built-in way to subscribe to a subset of the context.

// Problem: UserContext has name, email, preferences, notifications...
const UserContext = createContext(null);

// When notifications update, EVERY consumer re-renders
// Even components that only use name and never display notifications
function Header() {
  const { name } = useContext(UserContext); // Re-renders on ANY context change
  return <h1>Welcome, {name}</h1>;
}

The fix: split contexts. Instead of one big UserContext, create UserProfileContext, UserPreferencesContext, and NotificationsContext. Components only subscribe to what they need. This is more boilerplate, but it's correct — each context change only re-renders the components that care about that specific data.

When Context is the right choice

  • Theme, locale, auth status — values that change infrequently
  • Provider patterns where you need to inject dependencies
  • Small, well-scoped data that multiple components at different depths need

When Context is the wrong choice

  • Frequently updating data (typing in inputs, animations, real-time data)
  • Large stores where different consumers need different slices
  • When you need selector-based subscriptions (only re-render when this specific field changes)

Zustand: Minimal Global State

Zustand is what you reach for when Context gets painful. It's a tiny (1KB) store with selector-based subscriptions — meaning components only re-render when the specific data they select changes.

// Define a store
import { create } from 'zustand';

interface ArticleStore {
  articles: Article[];
  filters: Filters;
  setFilters: (filters: Filters) => void;
  addArticle: (article: Article) => void;
  removeArticle: (id: string) => void;
}

const useArticleStore = create<ArticleStore>((set) => ({
  articles: [],
  filters: { category: 'all', sort: 'date' },

  setFilters: (filters) => set({ filters }),

  addArticle: (article) => set((state) => ({
    articles: [...state.articles, article],
  })),

  removeArticle: (id) => set((state) => ({
    articles: state.articles.filter(a => a.id !== id),
  })),
}));
// Using selectors — only re-renders when filters change
function FilterBar() {
  const filters = useArticleStore(state => state.filters);
  const setFilters = useArticleStore(state => state.setFilters);

  return (
    <select
      value={filters.category}
      onChange={e => setFilters({ ...filters, category: e.target.value })}
    >
      <option value="all">All</option>
      <option value="politics">Politics</option>
      <option value="tech">Technology</option>
    </select>
  );
}

// Only re-renders when the articles array changes
function ArticleCount() {
  const count = useArticleStore(state => state.articles.length);
  return <span>{count} articles</span>;
}

Zustand's killer feature is selectors. In Context, if anything in the value object changes, every consumer re-renders. In Zustand, useArticleStore(state => state.articles.length) only re-renders when the count changes — not when an article's title is edited, not when filters change, not when anything else in the store updates. This is the granular reactivity that Context lacks.

Zustand with middleware

// persist middleware — automatic localStorage sync
import { persist } from 'zustand/middleware';

const useSettingsStore = create(
  persist<SettingsStore>(
    (set) => ({
      theme: 'dark',
      fontSize: 16,
      setTheme: (theme) => set({ theme }),
      setFontSize: (size) => set({ fontSize: size }),
    }),
    {
      name: 'user-settings', // localStorage key
    }
  )
);

// devtools middleware — Redux DevTools integration
import { devtools } from 'zustand/middleware';

const useStore = create(
  devtools(
    (set) => ({ /* ... */ }),
    { name: 'ArticleStore' }
  )
);

Jotai: Atomic State

Jotai takes a fundamentally different approach. Instead of a single store, you define individual atoms — independent pieces of state. Components subscribe to specific atoms. It's bottom-up state instead of top-down.

// Define atoms (pieces of state)
import { atom, useAtom } from 'jotai';

const articlesAtom = atom<Article[]>([]);
const filterAtom = atom<string>('all');

// Derived atom — computed from other atoms (like a spreadsheet formula)
const filteredArticlesAtom = atom((get) => {
  const articles = get(articlesAtom);
  const filter = get(filterAtom);
  return filter === 'all'
    ? articles
    : articles.filter(a => a.category === filter);
});

// Async atom — fetches data
const userAtom = atom(async () => {
  const res = await fetch('/api/user');
  return res.json();
});

// Write-only atom — actions
const addArticleAtom = atom(
  null,
  (get, set, article: Article) => {
    const current = get(articlesAtom);
    set(articlesAtom, [...current, article]);
  }
);
// Usage — subscribe to exactly what you need
function FilteredList() {
  const [filtered] = useAtom(filteredArticlesAtom);
  return filtered.map(a => <Card key={a.id} article={a} />);
}

function FilterSelect() {
  const [filter, setFilter] = useAtom(filterAtom);
  return <select value={filter} onChange={e => setFilter(e.target.value)}>...</select>;
}

Jotai vs Zustand mental model: Zustand is a single store with selectors (like a database you query). Jotai is a collection of atoms (like spreadsheet cells that can reference each other). Zustand is better when your state is a coherent object. Jotai is better when state is scattered and highly interdependent.

The Signals Conversation

Signals (used by Solid, Angular, Preact, and the proposed TC39 standard) represent a different reactivity model. Instead of re-running component functions and diffing, signals track which parts of the UI depend on which data and update them directly.

// Signals (conceptual — Solid.js syntax)
const [count, setCount] = createSignal(0);

// This component never "re-renders" in the React sense.
// The text node that reads count() updates directly.
function Counter() {
  return <div>Count: {count()}</div>;
}

In React's model, changing state re-runs the entire component function. In a signals model, changing a signal only updates the specific DOM text node that reads it. No diffing, no virtual DOM, no component re-execution.

Why React doesn't use signals (yet): The React team has explicitly said they considered and rejected signals for React's core model. Their argument: re-running component functions is simpler to reason about, easier to debug (just add console.log), and compatible with Server Components. Signals require a compiler or a runtime subscription tracker. React's bet is that the overhead of re-rendering is acceptable for the simplicity it provides. This is a genuine architectural tradeoff, not a technical limitation.

The Decision Tree

When you're staring at a new piece of state, ask these questions in order:

  1. Does it come from the server? → React Query / SWR / RSC. Don't store server data in client state.
  2. Is it in the URL? → useSearchParams. Filters, pagination, sort order — keep them in the URL so they're shareable and back-button-friendly.
  3. Does only one component use it? → useState. Don't prematurely lift state.
  4. Do a parent and its children share it? → Lift to the parent, pass via props.
  5. Do distant components share it? → Is the data updated infrequently? Context. Frequently? Zustand or Jotai.
  6. Is it complex interdependent state? → useReducer (local) or Zustand with slices (global).

The investigative journalist principle: The simplest explanation that fits all the evidence is usually correct. The simplest state management that handles your requirements is usually best. Don't reach for Zustand when useState works. Don't reach for Redux when Zustand works. Start simple and escalate only when you have a concrete problem, not an imagined future problem.

Check Your Understanding

1. Why does Context re-render all consumers when the value changes?

Context doesn't support selectors. When the Provider's value changes (by reference), React re-renders every component that calls useContext for that context. There's no mechanism to say "only wake me if this specific field changed." This is by design — Context optimizes for simplicity, not granular updates.

2. What makes Zustand's selectors better than Context for frequently-updating state?

Zustand selectors let each component subscribe to a slice of the store. The component only re-renders if its selected slice changes (compared with Object.is). A component selecting state.articles.length won't re-render when state.filters changes. Context has no equivalent — any change re-renders everything.

3. When would you choose Jotai over Zustand?

When your state is naturally composed of many independent pieces that derive from each other (like a spreadsheet). Jotai's atomic model makes derived state trivial: a derived atom automatically updates when its dependencies change. Zustand is better for coherent state objects where you think of the store as one unit.

4. You're building a news app with articles from an API, user preferences, and UI toggles. Where does each go?

Articles: React Query (server state, needs caching/refetching). User preferences: Zustand with persist middleware (infrequent updates, needs localStorage). UI toggles (modal open, sidebar collapsed): useState in the component that owns it. Filter/sort from URL: useSearchParams.

Exercise: Refactor State Management

  1. Take an existing project where you use Context for a store. Profile the re-renders using React DevTools. Count how many components re-render when you update one field.
  2. Refactor to Zustand with selectors. Profile again. Compare the render counts.
  3. Build a small dashboard with three independent data sources. Use Jotai atoms that derive from each other. A "total" atom should auto-update when any source changes.
← Lesson 03: Hooks Deep Dive Lesson 05: Server Components →
